## [00:00 - 03:08]

The title of this live interview tonight is How to Grow Your Online Customer and Prospect List 4-7 Times Faster Without Spending a Dime More on Traffic or Hiring some Hot Shot Copywriter to Do It for You. My name is Ben Settle of BenSettle.com and I am interviewing Daniel Levis. Daniel specializes in writing online promotions for information publishers, primarily in the health, financial, self-help and business opportunity fields. Daniel's helps both large direct marketers as well as hundreds of small home-based entrepreneurs to use the Internet to sell more effectively and has also personally trained and mentored a number of up-and-coming info marketers and copywriters. In the financial field he's worked with the Safe Money Report, Real Wealth Report, Gold Newsletter, the New Orleans Investment Conference, Stealth Stocks Online, Hidden Values Alert and a whole bunch of other well-known publications. In the BizApps and self-help field in the health markets, he's worked extensively with folks like Mark Victor Hanson, Robert Allen, Dr. Sevan Sinatra and a whole bunch of others. He's also collaborated with a lot of top-flight marketing educators like Joe Vitale, Yannick Silver, John Carlton, Clayton Makepeace and Dan Kennedy to produce several highly acclaimed marketing and copywriting info products. And he's been exposed to and he's overseen dozens of high-traffic scientific web marketing experiments that have allowed him to witness firsthand what works online and what's not working. Today he's going to share with you a simple step-by-step system that he says can exponentially increase the sales and profits of virtually any business. Daniel, welcome to the call my friend. Well thanks for having me Ben. I really appreciate the opportunity and I've come bearing gifts. Well good, you know we're all like gifts. Yeah, everybody on this call is going to have a chance to get a special report that I put together recently with Paul Hartunian and I know you're a big believer in Paul's work Ben. I am probably his biggest fan. I can't say enough good things. I think everybody should be using his stuff. Paul's strategies are not only great for getting featured on the radio and in print, newspapers and magazines and so forth, but they're really great for generating website traffic as well. And in addition to this material that Paul and I put together, ten lucky individuals will also have an opportunity to grab a fantastic package of copywriting tools and personal support worth over $2,200. And it ties right in directly with the system that we're going to be discussing here today. And I'm going to give away both of these insane value resources absolutely free. So hang around till the very end of the call folks when I tell you how you can get them. So Ben, I'm thrilled to have a chance to hang out with you and your members. My mission here is really to give everybody listening to this call some real neat, actionable information that they can use immediately

## [03:08 - 06:16]

to begin growing their customer and prospect lists faster. I want to give you something that you can use right after this call to put more money in your pocket starting tomorrow. I mean, I'm familiar with you and most people are familiar with your name, but how did you get into the business of helping people to use their websites more effectively to sell their products and services? Well, my previous career was in direct selling. Up until I started my marketing consulting business in late 2004, I made my living in a corporate environment selling high-tech services to Fortune 500 companies. And I started out on the Street Commission in the early 90s doing that. And you know, there's something about having to sell, Ben, or your family doesn't eat that has a way of compelling you to figure things out pretty quickly. And so I became a very serious student of sales psychology early on, and I began immersing myself in books and cassette tapes and so forth produced by direct selling giants like Tom Hopkins and Jay Douglas Edwards, Mark Victor Hansen, Frank Betker, and other folks like them who were just masters in the art of face-to-face persuasion. And then around 96, if I'm not mistaken, when voicemail started to hit critical mass, it started getting very difficult getting through to people on the telephone. I started writing lead generation letters, and I'd write these lead generation letters for myself and the other salespeople that I happened to be managing at the time that go out in the mail and a week or so later the phone would start ringing with new leads, and we would go out in person. We'd engage those new leads in the sales process. And then one day in 2003, my boss came in and he said that the company was rolling out a major new online initiative, complete with a fancy new website. And the plan was to tap into new markets that could be targeted more easily online, and they thought that they could double the number of leads they were giving us. And I said, great, what have you been waiting for? My income was largely performance-based, and I'd been pushing for that. So they went out and they hired an advertising agency, a web design company, IT consultants, and God knows who else. And about six months later, with great pomp and fanfare, the new website was ready to go live, and we all had great expectations that there would be a whole new source of leads that we could go out and close, and everybody was going to be happy and make a bunch of money. I mean, this thing is beautiful. Flash and videos, product demos, hundreds of pages of content, incredible design values, all of the bells and whistles. And they spent about $30,000 in banner ads and placements through a media company, and we waited. And we waited. And eventually, an itty little bitty, tiny trickle of leads started to drip into us about what we'd come to expect from a couple of hundred postcards dropped in the mail.

## [06:16 - 09:17]

These crowds had burned through six months and close to a quarter of a million bucks. And it was better than the corporate website that was getting hundreds of times the traffic. So I showed my boss my little skunk works website and the results. ## [12:18 - 15:25]

Here are the five Rs. The right person communicating the right message to the right audience at the right time and in the right way. Those are the five Rs. ## [27:38 - 30:45]

Now, as you know, Ben, the Internet is a very impersonal medium. It's actually one of its primary business advantages. Machines automate a good deal of the interaction required to service customers and sell them products and services. People come to your website, they fill out forms, they pay information, they even buy things. And all you do is pull a few levers and tweak a few dials, so to speak, now and then in the background and you can be completely invisible as a seller. However, our studies prove over and over again that people respond far more favorably. They buy significantly more frequently when they feel as though they're interacting at some level with a real human being. And I'm not talking about online chat or voiceover IP or any of that real-time stuff. All of that's fine, but it defeats the leverage of the Internet. I'm talking about a purely one-way web experience here where people are perfectly happy to suspend their disbelief and enter into a mental dialogue with you as you communicate your sales story on your website and in your emails and on your blog and in your videos and audios and so forth. What we've discovered is that by its very nature, the Internet demands that you project as much personality into your messages as humanly possible. So how do you do that? Well, audio, visual presentations, certainly one-way, and everything we've discussed here tonight is just as applicable to those types of media. But there's a whole lot you can do with static webpages as well. Our pass point to the following is being largely overlooked yet powerfully effective methods of injecting the human element into a static web page. Number one, picture yourself prominently on your web page, above or as close to the fold as possible, smiling, making a square eye contact with your online prospects when they arrive at your website, positioned to the left of and directly adjacent to the headline or opening body copy, shoulders profiling to the right and captioned appropriately. Or if you have to put your image for whatever reason on the right-hand side, you want to have it your shoulders profiling to the left. In other words, facing inward toward the copy. ## [36:51 - 39:55]

The Internet demands that you communicate your message in a series of linked steps. Each one pulling your prospect deeper into your overall message.
